topics.title: Write a program to find the largest of three numbers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCDL308 - PYTHON AND STATISTICAL MODELING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Write a program to find the largest of three numbers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Conditional Control Flow (KTU 2024 Definition):** Conditional statements in Python (`if`, `elif`, `else`) are selection control structures that evaluate Boolean expressions and execute specific blocks of code based on whether the condition evaluates to `True` or `False`. The problem of finding the **largest of three numbers** is the canonical introductory application of the **branching (decision-making) construct**, where pairwise relational comparisons are chained to identify the maximum element in a finite discrete set.

In the context of the KTU **PCCDL308 – Python and Statistical Modeling Lab (2024 Scheme)**, this experiment falls under **Module 2: Decision Control and Looping Constructs**, and tests the learner's mastery of:

- Relational operators (`>`, `<`, `>=`, `<=`, `==`, `!=`)
- Logical operators (`and`, `or`, `not`)
- The `if-elif-else` decision ladder
- Standard I/O using `input()` and `print()`
- Type casting (`int`, `float`)

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Tallest Student" Problem:**
> Imagine three students — Arjun (165 cm), Bindu (172 cm), and Chitra (168 cm) — standing in a row. To find the **tallest**, you would first compare Arjun and Bindu. Since Bindu is taller, you then compare Bindu with Chitra. Bindu is still taller, so Bindu is declared the winner.
> 
> This **"compare-the-winner-with-the-next"** strategy is exactly what the computer does with three numbers: it maintains a running "current best" candidate and updates it whenever a larger value is encountered.

## 1.3 Relational & Logical Operators Reference

| Operator | Meaning | Example | Result |
|---|---|---|---|
| `>` | Strictly greater than | `5 > 3` | `True` |
| `<` | Strictly less than | `5 < 3` | `False` |
| `>=` | Greater than or equal to | `5 >= 5` | `True` |
| `<=` | Less than or equal to | `4 <= 5` | `True` |
| `==` | Equal to | `5 == 5` | `True` |
| `!=` | Not equal to | `5 != 3` | `True` |
| `and` | Logical AND | `(5>3) and (5<10)` | `True` |
| `or` | Logical OR | `(5>3) or (5<1)` | `True` |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (CO2 – PCCDL308):** *“Apply decision control and looping structures to solve computational problems.”* The "largest of three" experiment is the **direct, primary assessment vehicle** for CO2 in most internal lab evaluations under the 2024 scheme.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Underlying Logic — Three Accepted Algorithmic Strategies

### Strategy A — The Sequential Comparison Ladder (`if-elif-else`)
This is the **canonical KTU-expected approach**. We compare `a` with `b` and `c` using chained conditions.

### Strategy B — The "Tournament Method" (Nested `if`)
Pair `a` and `b` first; the winner is then compared with `c`. This is the **most intuitive** method and mirrors the real-world analogy.

### Strategy C — Built-in `max()` Function
Python's built-in `max()` accepts multiple arguments and returns the largest, leveraging the **C-implemented CPython runtime** for optimized performance.

### Strategy D — The Ternary (Conditional) Expression
A compact one-liner using Python's conditional expression syntax: `value_if_true if condition else value_if_false`.

## 2.2 Mathematical Formulation

> [!NOTE]
> Given three real numbers $a$, $b$, $c \in \mathbb{R}$, the largest element $L$ is defined as:
> $$ L = \max(a, b, c) $$
> 
> More formally, using the **supremum operator**:
> $$ L = \sup\{a, b, c\} $$
> 
> Equivalently, $L$ is the unique value satisfying:
> $$ L \geq a, \quad L \geq b, \quad L \geq c $$
> 
> and there exists at least one $x \in \{a, b, c\}$ such that $L = x$.

## 2.3 KTU Formula / Cheat Sheet

| # | Approach | Pseudocode Logic | Best Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | `if-elif-else` ladder | If `a>=b and a>=c` → `a`; else if `b>=a and b>=c` → `b`; else → `c` | KTU board exam default |
| 2 | Nested `if` (tournament) | If `a>b`: if `a>c` → `a`, else → `c`; else: if `b>c` → `b`, else → `c` | Conceptual clarity |
| 3 | `max()` built-in | `max(a, b, c)` | Production code, shortest |
| 4 | Ternary expression | `(a if a>c else c) if a>b else (b if b>c else c)` | Compact scripting |
| 5 | Sorted approach | `sorted([a,b,c])[-1]` | List-based input |

## 2.4 Engineering Real-World Utility

- **Statistical Modeling**: Computing the maximum value in a sample window.
- **Sensor Networks**: Identifying peak readings in IoT data streams.
- **Database Systems**: SQL's `MAX()` aggregate is the direct analogue.
- **Machine Learning**: Hyperparameter tuning often requires finding the maximum accuracy across trials.
- **Control Systems**: Selecting the maximum-priority interrupt request (IRQ) in a microcontroller.

## 2.5 Boolean Algebra Behind Comparisons

The core compound condition `a >= b and a >= c` can be analyzed via the **truth table** of the AND operator:

| $a \geq b$ | $a \geq c$ | Result |
|---|---|---|
| True | True | True |
| True | False | False |
| False | True | False |
| False | False | False |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Common KTU Pitfall:** Beginners often write `if a > b and a > c:` (strict). If two or more numbers are **tied for the largest**, the strict version will misreport. The KTU-recommended pattern uses `>=` to correctly handle ties and report the **first** maximum encountered.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Approach 1 — The `if-elif-else` Ladder (Full Derivation)

We derive the three Boolean predicates that uniquely partition the decision space:

**Step 1:** Define the candidate set $\mathcal{C} = \{a, b, c\}$.

**Step 2:** Predicate $P_a$ — "$a$ is the maximum":
$$ P_a : (a \geq b) \wedge (a \geq c) $$

**Step 3:** Predicate $P_b$ — "$b$ is the maximum (and $a$ is not)":
$$ P_b : (b \geq a) \wedge (b \geq c) \wedge \neg P_a $$

**Step 4:** Predicate $P_c$ — "$c$ is the maximum (and neither $a$ nor $b$ is)":
$$ P_c : (c \geq a) \wedge (c \geq b) \wedge \neg P_a \wedge \neg P_b $$

**Step 5:** The complete decision ladder is therefore:
$$ L = \begin{cases} a, & \text{if } P_a \text{ is True} \\ b, & \text{else if } P_b \text{ is True} \\ c, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases} $$

## 3.2 Approach 2 — The Tournament (Nested `if`) Derivation

**Step 1:** Perform a pairwise comparison between $a$ and $b$:
$$ w_1 = \begin{cases} a, & \text{if } a \geq b \\ b, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases} $$

**Step 2:** Compare the winner $w_1$ with $c$:
$$ L = \begin{cases} w_1, & \text{if } w_1 \geq c \\ c, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases} $$

**Step 3:** Unrolling the substitution gives the equivalent expanded form:
$$ L = \begin{cases} a, & \text{if } (a \geq b) \wedge (a \geq c) \\ c, & \text{if } (a \geq b) \wedge (c > a) \\ b, & \text{if } (b > a) \wedge (b \geq c) \\ c, & \text{if } (b > a) \wedge (c > b) \end{cases} $$

This **nesting depth of 2** is the minimum possible for three-way comparison and is exactly what the KTU evaluator expects for the "largest of three" experiment.

## 3.3 Approach 3 — The `max()` Built-in (Mathematical Justification)

The built-in function `max(iterable)` is formally defined as:
$$ \max(\mathcal{C}) = \arg\max_{x \in \mathcal{C}} x $$

When called with multiple positional arguments, CPython internally reduces using pairwise `max()` calls:
$$ \max(a, b, c) = \max(\max(a, b), c) $$

This is mathematically equivalent to our nested-if derivation in Section 3.2.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Trace

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Lab Exam Trace (Recorded in Observation Book):**
> Let $a = 45$, $b = 78$, $c = 62$.

**Step 1:** Evaluate $P_a$ — Is $45 \geq 78$? **No** → $P_a$ is False.

**Step 2:** Evaluate $P_b$ — Is $78 \geq 45$ AND $78 \geq 62$?
- $78 \geq 45$ → True
- $78 \geq 62$ → True
- AND → **True**

**Step 3:** Output → `Largest number is 78`.

**Verification:** $78 \geq 45$ ✓, $78 \geq 62$ ✓, $78 \in \{45, 78, 62\}$ ✓. Therefore $L = 78$. ✅

## 3.5 Edge Case Analysis Matrix

| Input | Expected Output | `if-elif-else` | `max()` |
|---|---|---|---|
| `(5, 5, 5)` | 5 | 5 ✓ | 5 ✓ |
| `(-1, -5, -3)` | -1 | -1 ✓ | -1 ✓ |
| `(10, 10, 2)` | 10 (tie) | 10 ✓ | 10 ✓ |
| `(0, 0, 0.001)` | 0.001 | 0.001 ✓ | 0.001 ✓ |
| `(1.5, 2.5, 2.5)` | 2.5 (first occurrence) | 2.5 ✓ | 2.5 ✓ |
| `('a', 'b', 'c')` (strings) | `'c'` | `'c'` ✓ | `'c'` ✓ |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Flowchart — The Tournament (Nested `if`) Method

This is the **exact diagram KTU examiners expect in the observation record** for Module Experiment 2.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA([Start]) --> inputA[/Read a, b, c/]
    inputA --> checkA{a > b ?}
    checkA -- Yes --> checkB{a > c ?}
    checkA -- No --> checkC{b > c ?}
    checkB -- Yes --> printA[/Print a is largest/]
    checkB -- No --> printB[/Print c is largest/]
    checkC -- Yes --> printC[/Print b is largest/]
    checkC -- No --> printD[/Print c is largest/]
    printA --> stopZ([End])
    printB --> stopZ
    printC --> stopZ
    printD --> stopZ
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — The `if-elif-else` Ladder Method

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startB([Start]) --> inputB[/Read a, b, c/]
    inputB --> cond1{Condition: a is greater than b AND a is greater than c}
    cond1 -- True --> outA[/Output: a is the largest/]
    cond1 -- False --> cond2{Condition: b is greater than a AND b is greater than c}
    cond2 -- True --> outB[/Output: b is the largest/]
    cond2 -- False --> outC[/Output: c is the largest/]
    outA --> endB([End])
    outB --> endB
    outC --> endB
```

## 4.3 Complete Python Source Code — KTU Lab Reference Implementation

```python
# ============================================================
# Experiment 2: Largest of Three Numbers
# Course: PCCDL308 - Python and Statistical Modeling Lab
# KTU 2024 Scheme | Module 2: Decision Control
# ============================================================

# ---------- Approach 1: if-elif-else Ladder (KTU Default) ----------
a = int(input("Enter the first number: "))
b = int(input("Enter the second number: "))
c = int(input("Enter the third number: "))

if (a >= b) and (a >= c):
    largest = a
elif (b >= a) and (b >= c):
    largest = b
else:
    largest = c

print("The largest number is:", largest)


# ---------- Approach 2: Nested if (Tournament Method) ----------
if a >= b:
    if a >= c:
        print("The largest number is:", a)
    else:
        print("The largest number is:", c)
else:
    if b >= c:
        print("The largest number is:", b)
    else:
        print("The largest number is:", c)


# ---------- Approach 3: Built-in max() ----------
print("The largest number is:", max(a, b, c))
```

### 4.3.1 Sample Input / Output Trace

| Line | Input / Output |
|---|---|
| Input 1 | `Enter the first number: 45` |
| Input 2 | `Enter the second number: 78` |
| Input 3 | `Enter the third number: 62` |
| Output | `The largest number is: 78` |
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A.1 — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]`
**Differentiate between the `if-elif-else` ladder and the nested `if` construct for finding the largest of three numbers. Which is computationally more efficient and why?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- The `if-elif-else` ladder uses a **flat (sequential)** structure where conditions are evaluated top-down, stopping at the first `True`. In the worst case, it requires **3 comparisons** (e.g., $a \geq b$, $a \geq c$, $b \geq a$, $b \geq c$).
- The **nested `if`** structure performs a pairwise "tournament": first comparing $a$ and $b$ (1 comparison), then comparing the winner with $c$ (1 more comparison), for a worst case of **2 comparisons**.
- **Therefore, the nested `if` is more efficient** with $O(1)$ time in both cases, but with fewer total relational operations on average.

### Question A.2 — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**What is the output of the following Python code? Justify.**
```python
a, b, c = 10, 10, 5
if a >= b and a >= c:
    print("a")
elif b >= a and b >= c:
    print("b")
else:
    print("c")
```

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- The output is **`a`**.
- **Justification:** The first condition `a >= b and a >= c` evaluates as `(10 >= 10) and (10 >= 5)` → `True and True` → `True`. Hence the program prints `"a"` and skips the remaining branches. This demonstrates the **first-true-wins** semantics of the `elif` ladder, even when ties exist.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question B.1 (A) — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**(a) [7 Marks]** Write a complete Python program to input three integers from the user and print the **largest** and the **smallest** of the three using only `if-elif-else` (no built-in `min()` or `max()`). Display appropriate messages.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Modify the program to also handle the case where **all three numbers are equal**, and print a special message `"All numbers are equal"`. Also, handle non-integer input gracefully using `try-except`.

### Model Solution

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

```python
a = int(input("Enter first number: "))
b = int(input("Enter second number: "))
c = int(input("Enter third number: "))

# Largest
if a >= b and a >= c:
    largest = a
elif b >= a and b >= c:
    largest = b
else:
    largest = c

# Smallest
if a <= b and a <= c:
    smallest = a
elif b <= a and b <= c:
    smallest = b
else:
    smallest = c

print("Largest number is:", largest)
print("Smallest number is:", smallest)
```

**Mark Allocation:**
- Correct input with `int()` conversion — **1 mark**
- Largest logic with proper compound condition — **3 marks**
- Smallest logic with proper compound condition — **2 marks**
- Correct `print()` output formatting — **1 mark**

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

```python
try:
    a = int(input("Enter first number: "))
    b = int(input("Enter second number: "))
    c = int(input("Enter third number: "))

    if a == b == c:
        print("All numbers are equal")
    else:
        if a >= b and a >= c:
            largest = a
        elif b >= a and b >= c:
            largest = b
        else:
            largest = c

        if a <= b and a <= c:
            smallest = a
        elif b <= a and b <= c:
            smallest = b
        else:
            smallest = c

        print("Largest number is:", largest)
        print("Smallest number is:", smallest)
except ValueError:
    print("Invalid input! Please enter integers only.")
```

**Mark Allocation:**
- Equality check `a == b == c` — **1 mark**
- Correct message handling — **1 mark**
- Reuse of largest/smallest logic inside `else` — **3 marks**
- `try-except` for `ValueError` — **2 marks**

---

### Question B.1 (B) — `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]` (Internal Choice)
**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the difference between **relational operators** and **logical operators** in Python. Give two examples of each, demonstrating their use in finding the largest of three numbers.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Write a Python program that takes three numbers as input and prints them in **descending order** (largest to smallest) using `if-elif-else`. Show the output for the input `25, 9, 47`.

### Model Solution

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Relational Operators** compare two values and return a Boolean:

| Operator | Meaning | Example in context |
|---|---|---|
| `>` | Greater than | `a > b` |
| `>=` | Greater than or equal | `a >= b` |

**Logical Operators** combine Boolean expressions:

| Operator | Meaning | Example in context |
|---|---|---|
| `and` | Both must be True | `(a > b) and (a > c)` |
| `or` | At least one True | `(a > b) or (a > c)` |

**Demonstration in code:**
```python
# Relational example
if a > b:           # Uses relational >
    print("a > b")

# Logical example
if (a > b) and (a > c):   # Uses logical 'and'
    print("a is the largest")
```

**Mark Allocation:**
- Definitions of both operator types — **2 marks**
- Two examples of each — **2 marks**
- Demonstration in "largest of three" context — **3 marks**

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

```python
a = int(input("Enter first number: "))
b = int(input("Enter second number: "))
c = int(input("Enter third number: "))

if a >= b and a >= c:
    if b >= c:
        print("Descending order:", a, b, c)
    else:
        print("Descending order:", a, c, b)
elif b >= a and b >= c:
    if a >= c:
        print("Descending order:", b, a, c)
    else:
        print("Descending order:", b, c, a)
else:
    if a >= b:
        print("Descending order:", c, a, b)
    else:
        print("Descending order:", c, b, a)
```

**For input `25, 9, 47`:**
- `a=25`, `b=9`, `c=47`
- Condition `a >= b and a >= c` → `25 >= 9 (T) and 25 >= 47 (F)` → False
- Condition `b >= a and b >= c` → `9 >= 25 (F) ...` → False
- Goes to `else`: `a >= b` → `25 >= 9` → True → prints `47, 25, 9`
- **Output:** `Descending order: 47 25 9`

**Mark Allocation:**
- Correct nested structure — **3 marks**
- All 6 ordering permutations handled — **2 marks**
- Correct output for sample input — **2 marks**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Strict vs. Non-Strict Inequality:** Using `>` instead of `>=` causes the program to **miss the largest** when two or more numbers tie (e.g., input `5, 5, 3` would incorrectly output `3`).
> 2. **Missing Parentheses in Compound Conditions:** Writing `if a >= b and a >= c:` without parentheses is *valid* but KTU evaluators prefer `if (a >= b) and (a >= c):` for explicit clarity. Both are accepted; consistency matters.
> 3. **Forgetting `int()` / `float()` Casting:** `input()` always returns a **string**. Comparing two strings like `"45" > "78"` performs **lexicographic (dictionary) comparison**, not numerical — `"78" < "9"` because `'7' < '9'`! This is the **#1 cause** of "wrong output" in KTU lab exams.
> 4. **IndentationError:** Python is **whitespace-sensitive**. The KTU auto-grader for online submissions fails programs that mix tabs and spaces.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Board Exam:**

- ✅ The problem of finding the **largest of three numbers** maps directly to the mathematical operator $\max(a, b, c)$.
- ✅ Three primary approaches: **`if-elif-else` ladder**, **nested `if` (tournament)**, and **built-in `max()`**.
- ✅ Always use **non-strict inequality `>=`** to correctly handle ties.
- ✅ **Compound conditions** require logical operators: `(a >= b) and (a >= c)`.
- ✅ `input()` returns a **string** → must be type-cast using `int()` or `float()` before comparison.
- ✅ The **tournament method** uses only **2 comparisons** in the worst case; the ladder may use up to **4 comparisons**.
- ✅ For tie cases (`a == b == c`), all approaches correctly return that value due to `>=` semantics.
- ✅ **Indentation** is syntactically mandatory in Python — use **4 spaces** per level (PEP 8).
- ✅ The built-in `max()` is the most concise but **discouraged** in KTU exams when the question explicitly tests decision constructs.
- ✅ CO2 of PCCDL308 is **directly assessed** by this experiment in both **continuous evaluation (lab record + viva)** and **ESE practical**.
- ✅ Always include a **flowchart** in your lab record — it carries **2–3 marks** on its own.
- ✅ Sample trace with three different test cases (positive, negative, equal) is **mandatory** in the observation book.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Write a program to print the multiplication table of a number n
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCDL308 - PYTHON AND STATISTICAL MODELING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Write a program to print the multiplication table of a number n

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **multiplication table program** is a fundamental iterative construct in Python that generates and displays the successive products of a given integer $n$ with multipliers ranging from $1$ to a specified upper limit $m$ (conventionally $m = 10$). Mathematically, it computes and prints the sequence:

$$T(n) = \{n \times i \mid i \in \mathbb{Z}, 1 \leq i \leq m\}$$

In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework for **PCCDL308 (Python and Statistical Modeling Lab)**, this experiment falls under **Course Outcome CO1**: *Write simple Python programs using fundamental programming constructs including variables, data types, operators, control structures, and loops.*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2 - Python Basics):**
> This experiment tests a student's ability to:
> 1. Accept user input using the `input()` function.
> 2. Apply type casting (`int()`, `float()`, `str()`).
> 3. Implement loop control structures (`for`, `while`).
> 4. Use the `print()` function with formatted output (f-strings / `format()`).
> 5. Apply arithmetic operators in a real computational context.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The School Times-Table Chart:**
> Imagine you are a Class 3 student with a laminated chart stuck on your study desk. The chart for the number **7** reads: `7 × 1 = 7`, `7 × 2 = 14`, `7 × 3 = 21`, ... up to `7 × 10 = 70`. A teacher reads out the multiplier (`1`, `2`, `3`...) and you write the answer.
>
> In this experiment, **Python plays the role of the student**, and the **multipliers are spoken automatically by a `for` loop**. The program "writes" each line of the chart on the console, line by line, exactly as you would on a notebook.

### 1.3 Key Standard Metrics and Constants

- **Standard range limit:** $m = 10$ (as per conventional primary mathematics and default in most KTU model answers).
- **Data type of $n$:** `int` (integer) — required for exact arithmetic; if `float`, output remains numerically correct but is conventionally avoided in KTU solutions.
- **Operator precedence:** Multiplication (`*`) has higher precedence than addition, so parentheses are unnecessary around `n * i`.
- **Unicode alignment:** Use consistent spacing in `print()` to keep the output neatly column-aligned (a common KTU evaluator expectation).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Logical Breakdown — The Operational Concept

The multiplication table is built upon three primary computational pillars:

1. **Input Phase (Acquisition)**
   - The program prompts the user via `input()`.
   - The returned string is converted to an integer using `int()` type casting.
   - Edge case: non-numeric input raises a `ValueError` (advanced handling, often asked in viva).

2. **Processing Phase (Iteration)**
   - A counter variable $i$ iterates from $1$ to $m$ (typically $10$).
   - At each step, the product $P = n \times i$ is computed using the multiplication operator `*`.
   - This is a classic **definite iteration** scenario — `for` loop is preferred for clarity.

3. **Output Phase (Display)**
   - The `print()` function emits formatted text for each $i$.
   - Formatted string literals (f-strings, PEP 498) are the modern, KTU-recommended approach.
   - The newline character `\n` is implicitly added by `print()` after each invocation.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Construct | Syntax | Purpose in This Program | Example for $n=5$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| Input | `n = int(input("Enter n: "))` | Reads the table base | `5` |
| Range | `range(1, 11)` | Generates $1$ to $10$ (inclusive of $1$, exclusive of $11$) | `1,2,...,10` |
| For Loop | `for i in range(1, 11):` | Iterates through multipliers | — |
| Multiply | `n * i` | Computes the product $P$ | `5 * 3 = 15` |
| f-String | `f"{n} x {i} = {n*i}"` | Concatenates and displays the result | `5 x 3 = 15` |
| End Param | `print(..., end="\t")` | Keeps output on the same line (optional variant) | `5 x 3 = 15` |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The core arithmetic identity** that the program implements is:
> $$P_i = n \times i, \quad \text{for } i = 1, 2, 3, \dots, m$$
> This is a **linear function** of $i$ with slope $n$ and zero intercept. Hence the table forms an **arithmetic progression** with common difference $n$ in the product column.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Statistical Modeling:** Building lookup tables for discrete probability distributions.
- **Cryptography:** Generating modular multiplication tables in RSA key generation.
- **Signal Processing:** Computing convolution kernels (which are essentially small multiplication tables).
- **Numerical Methods:** Matrix multiplication subroutines trace back to scalar multiplication tables.
- **Compiler Design:** Lookup tables in optimization phases rely on such systematic enumeration.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Algorithm Derivation (Exhaustive Walkthrough)

We derive the program from the mathematical specification:

**Step 1 — Define the problem mathematically.**

We want to display the set:
$$S = \{(n, i, n \cdot i) \mid 1 \leq i \leq m\}$$

**Step 2 — Choose a data source.**

The variable $n$ must come from external input (the user), while $i$ is internally generated by a counter.

**Step 3 — Select the iteration strategy.**

Two options exist in Python:
- **Option A:** `for i in range(1, m+1)` — definite, clean, idiomatic.
- **Option B:** `while i <= m` with manual increment — more verbose, but tests loop understanding (often asked in viva).

**Step 4 — Formulate the output expression.**

For each $i$, we display a triple $(n, i, n \cdot i)$ in human-readable form:
$$\text{output}_i = f"\{n\} \times \{i\} = \{n \cdot i\}"$$

**Step 5 — Assemble the final program.**

### 3.2 Complete Reference Implementations

#### 3.2.1 Method 1 — Using `for` Loop (KTU Recommended)

```python
# Program to print the multiplication table of a number n
# KTU PCCDL308 - Module Experiment 2

# Step 1: Accept input
n = int(input("Enter a number: "))

# Step 2: Define table range
m = 10

# Step 3: Print the table header
print(f"\nMultiplication Table of {n}")
print("-" * 30)

# Step 4: Iterate and display
for i in range(1, m + 1):
    print(f"{n} x {i} = {n * i}")
```

**Sample Output (for n = 7):**

```
Enter a number: 7

Multiplication Table of 7
------------------------------
7 x 1 = 7
7 x 2 = 14
7 x 3 = 21
7 x 4 = 28
7 x 5 = 35
7 x 6 = 42
7 x 7 = 49
7 x 8 = 56
7 x 9 = 63
7 x 10 = 70
```

#### 3.2.2 Method 2 — Using `while` Loop (Viva Favorite)

```python
n = int(input("Enter a number: "))
i = 1
while i <= 10:
    print(f"{n} * {i} = {n * i}")
    i += 1
```

#### 3.2.3 Method 3 — Using List Comprehension (Advanced / Bonus Credit)

```python
n = int(input("Enter a number: "))
[print(f"{n} x {i} = {n*i}") for i in range(1, 11)]
```

#### 3.2.4 Method 4 — Formatted Columnar Output (Range up to 20)

```python
n = int(input("Enter a number: "))
print(f"{'-'*25}\n Multiplication Table of {n}\n{'-'*25}")
for i in range(1, 21):
    print(f" {n:>3}  x  {i:>3}  =  {n*i:>5}")
```

### 3.3 Worked Example — Dry Run for $n = 5$

Let us trace the execution step-by-step:

| Iteration ($i$) | Computation $n \times i$ | Output Line |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 1 | $5 \times 1 = 5$ | `5 x 1 = 5` |
| 2 | $5 \times 2 = 10$ | `5 x 2 = 10` |
| 3 | $5 \times 3 = 15$ | `5 x 3 = 15` |
| 4 | $5 \times 4 = 20$ | `5 x 4 = 20` |
| 5 | $5 \times 5 = 25$ | `5 x 5 = 25` |
| 6 | $5 \times 6 = 30$ | `5 x 6 = 30` |
| 7 | $5 \times 7 = 35$ | `5 x 7 = 35` |
| 8 | $5 \times 8 = 40$ | `5 x 8 = 40` |
| 9 | $5 \times 9 = 45$ | `5 x 9 = 45` |
| 10 | $5 \times 10 = 50$ | `5 x 10 = 50` |

The sequence of products $\{5, 10, 15, 20, 25, 30, 35, 40, 45, 50\}$ is an **arithmetic progression (AP)** with:
- First term $a = n \times 1 = 5$
- Common difference $d = n = 5$
- $k$-th term formula: $P_k = n \cdot k = 5k$

### 3.4 Common Variations Tested in KTU Labs

1. **Print tables from 1 to $n$** (nested loop) — different experiment, but related.
2. **Reverse table** ($i$ from $10$ to $1$): `range(10, 0, -1)`.
3. **Range specified by user** (`m` accepted as input).
4. **Formatted two-column layout** (e.g., `5x1=5\t6x1=6` on the same line).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Flowchart — Program Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A([Start]) --> B[/Read input n/]
    B --> C{n is numeric?}
    C -- No --> E[/Print Error: Invalid input/]
    E --> Z([End])
    C -- Yes --> D[Convert n to int]
    D --> F[Initialize i = 1]
    F --> G{i <= 10?}
    G -- No --> Z
    G -- Yes --> H[Compute product = n * i]
    H --> I[/Print n x i = product/]
    I --> J[Increment i = i + 1]
    J --> G
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[User Input Module] --> B[Type Casting Module]
    B --> C[Iteration Controller]
    C --> D[Arithmetic Compute Unit]
    D --> E[Formatted Output Module]
    E --> F[(Console / Terminal)]
```

### 4.3 Control Flow Sequence (for-loop version)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant U as User
    participant P as Python Interpreter
    participant O as Output Stream
    U->>P: Enter n
    P->>P: int(n) conversion
    loop For i in 1 to 10
        P->>P: product = n * i
        P->>O: print formatted line
    end
    P->>U: Table displayed
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model]**
*What is the role of the `range()` function in a Python `for` loop? Explain with reference to a program that prints the multiplication table of a number.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The `range()` function generates a sequence of integers that controls the number of iterations of a `for` loop. It has three forms:
- `range(stop)` — yields `0, 1, ..., stop-1`
- `range(start, stop)` — yields `start, start+1, ..., stop-1`
- `range(start, stop, step)` — yields `start, start+step, ...` up to but excluding `stop`.

In a multiplication table program, `range(1, 11)` generates the multipliers $1, 2, \ldots, 10$. The loop variable $i$ takes each of these values one at a time, and the product $n \times i$ is printed for each. Since the upper bound is exclusive, `range(1, 11)` correctly produces 10 iterations, satisfying $1 \leq i \leq 10$.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model]**
*Differentiate between the `for` loop and the `while` loop in Python. Which is more suitable for printing a fixed-range multiplication table, and why?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
| Aspect | `for` loop | `while` loop |
|---|---|---|
| Usage | Iterates over a sequence (e.g., `range`) | Repeats while a condition is `True` |
| Initialization | Built into the `for` statement | Must be done before the loop |
| Termination | Automatic when sequence ends | Manual via condition or `break` |
| Risk of infinite loop | None (for finite iterables) | Possible if condition never becomes `False` |

For a fixed-range multiplication table (1 to 10), the `for` loop is **more suitable** because:
1. The number of iterations is known in advance ($10$).
2. It is more concise and idiomatic.
3. There is no risk of forgetting to update the counter.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Missing type cast:** Writing `n = input("...")` and then using `n * i` directly will cause a `TypeError` (string multiplication). This is a **2-mark deduction** in KTU labs.
> 2. **Off-by-one error in `range()`:** Using `range(1, 10)` instead of `range(1, 11)` produces only 9 lines. Examiners deduct **1 mark**.
> 3. **No input prompt or unclear output format:** Always include a clear prompt and a header. Unformatted outputs (e.g., just `5 10 15`) lose **1–2 marks** for lack of readability.
> 4. **Not including the `int()` conversion explicitly** is treated as incomplete logic — **2 marks lost**.
> 5. **Hardcoding `n` instead of using `input()`** is a common mistake — the program is then not a "general" table printer. **3 marks lost**.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - Model 2024]**

**(a)** Write a Python program to accept an integer $n$ from the user and print its multiplication table from $1$ to $10$ using a `for` loop. Include proper input prompts and formatted output. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Modify the program in part (a) to accept a custom range $m$ from the user (i.e., print the table from $1$ to $m$) and display the products in right-aligned columns. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) [7 marks]**
```python
# Multiplication Table - Part (a)
n = int(input("Enter an integer: "))
print(f"\nMultiplication Table of {n}")
print("-" * 30)
for i in range(1, 11):
    print(f"{n} x {i} = {n * i}")
```
**Mark distribution:**
- Input and type cast — 2 marks
- `for` loop with correct range — 2 marks
- Formatted `print` — 2 marks
- Output style and indentation — 1 mark

**(b) [7 marks]**
```python
# Multiplication Table - Part (b) with custom range
n = int(input("Enter an integer: "))
m = int(input("Enter the range: "))
print(f"\nMultiplication Table of {n} (1 to {m})")
print("-" * 35)
for i in range(1, m + 1):
    print(f" {n:>4}  x  {i:>4}  =  {n*i:>6}")
```
**Mark distribution:**
- Accepting second input `m` — 1 mark
- Updating `range(1, m+1)` — 2 marks
- Column alignment using `:>w` format specifier — 3 marks
- Header and visual formatting — 1 mark

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - Model 2024, Alternative]**

**(a)** Write a Python program to print the multiplication table of a number $n$ using a `while` loop. The program should validate that the input is a positive integer; otherwise, it should re-prompt the user. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain, with code, how you would extend the program in part (a) to print multiplication tables of all numbers from $1$ to $N$ (a nested loop scenario). Show the sample output structure. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) [7 marks]**
```python
# Multiplication Table using while loop with validation
while True:
    try:
        n = int(input("Enter a positive integer: "))
        if n <= 0:
            print("Please enter a number greater than 0.")
            continue
        break
    except ValueError:
        print("Invalid input. Please enter an integer.")

print(f"\nMultiplication Table of {n}")
print("-" * 30)
i = 1
while i <= 10:
    print(f"{n} x {i} = {n * i}")
    i += 1
```
**Mark distribution:**
- `while True` validation loop with `try-except` — 3 marks
- Positive integer check — 1 mark
- `while i <= 10` with manual increment — 2 marks
- Clean output — 1 mark

**(b) [7 marks]**
```python
# Tables of all numbers from 1 to N
N = int(input("Enter N: "))
for n in range(1, N + 1):
    print(f"\n=== Table of {n} ===")
    for i in range(1, 11):
        print(f"{n} x {i} = {n * i}")
```
**Mark distribution:**
- Outer `for n` loop — 2 marks
- Inner `for i` loop — 2 marks
- Section header for each table — 2 marks
- Correctness of nested structure — 1 mark

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [ ] A multiplication table is the set of products $n \times i$ for $i = 1, \ldots, m$.
- [ ] Always use `int(input(...))` — never omit the type cast.
- [ ] `range(1, 11)` generates $1$ to $10$; remember the **upper bound is exclusive**.
- [ ] f-strings (`f"{n} x {i} = {n*i}"`) are the **KTU-preferred** output style.
- [ ] For definite iteration, prefer `for`; for conditional repetition, use `while`.
- [ ] Off-by-one errors in `range()` and missing type casts are the **top two reasons for losing marks**.
- [ ] Right-alignment using `:>w` makes the output neat and earns aesthetic credit.
- [ ] Always include a prompt string in `input()` and a clear header before the table.
- [ ] The product sequence is an **arithmetic progression** with common difference $n$.
- [ ] Viva questions: Why `range(1, 11)` and not `range(1, 10)`? What happens if `input()` is not cast? What is PEP 498?

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Write a program to find surface area and volume of a cylinder using function
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCDL308 - PYTHON AND STATISTICAL MODELING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Write a program to find surface area and volume of a cylinder using function

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Cylinder?

A **cylinder** is a three-dimensional (3D) solid geometric shape bounded by two parallel circular bases of equal radius connected by a curved lateral surface. In everyday life, a cylinder appears in objects like a water pipe, a cold drink can, a gas cylinder, a pencil, a pillar, and even a candle.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:** A cylinder is a solid of revolution generated by rotating a rectangle about one of its sides. It is defined completely by two parameters: **radius (r)** of the circular base and **height (h)** of the solid.

## 1.2 What Does "Using Function" Mean in Python?

In the context of this experiment, the phrase **"using function"** means we must define and call **user-defined functions** in Python to perform the calculation. A function is a reusable, named block of code that takes **inputs (parameters)**, processes them, and returns an **output (return value)**.

In Python, a function is created using the keyword `def`.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy (Real-World Intuition)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Think of a function as a "vending machine" for calculations.**
> - You put in two coins (radius `r` and height `h`).
> - You press a button labeled "Volume" or "Surface Area".
> - The machine processes the input internally using the formula.
> - The output (coke/snacks) drops out — that's the returned value.

This is exactly what we do in Python: we hand the function two values, and it gives back the calculated area or volume.

## 1.4 Why Do We Need Functions?

| Reason | Explanation |
|---|---|
| **Reusability** | Write once, use many times. No code duplication. |
| **Modularity** | Each function handles one specific task (one formula). |
| **Readability** | The main code becomes short and self-explanatory. |
| **Easy Debugging** | If the volume is wrong, you only check the volume function. |
| **Testing** | Each function can be independently tested for correctness. |

## 1.5 The Two Formulas We Need

For a cylinder with radius `r` and height `h`:

- **Volume** = area of circular base × height
- **Surface Area** = area of the two bases + area of the curved side

We will derive these properly in Section 3 and write the Python program in the next sections.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Breakdown of the Mathematical Model

A cylinder is composed of three surfaces:

1. **Top circular base** — a flat circle of radius `r`.
2. **Bottom circular base** — another identical flat circle of radius `r`.
3. **Curved (lateral) surface** — the "wall" connecting the two circles.

> [!NOTE]
> KTU examiners often test whether students distinguish between **Curved Surface Area (CSA)** and **Total Surface Area (TSA)**. Knowing the difference is a guaranteed scoring area.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic (Why and How)

### Step 1 — Area of a Single Circular Base
A circle's area comes from the famous formula:
$$\text{Area of circle} = \pi r^{2}$$
The **why**: A circle can be thought of as a regular polygon with infinitely many sides. Integrating infinitesimally thin concentric rings of radius `r` and thickness `dr` from `0` to `R` gives the area.

### Step 2 — Volume of the Cylinder
Take the circular base of area `πr²` and "stack" it from height `0` to height `h`. The total swept volume is:
$$V = (\text{Base Area}) \times (\text{Height}) = \pi r^{2} h$$

### Step 3 — Curved (Lateral) Surface Area
Unroll the curved wall into a flat rectangle. The rectangle has:
- Width = circumference of the base = `2πr`
- Height = `h`

So:
$$\text{CSA} = 2 \pi r h$$

### Step 4 — Total Surface Area
Add the two base circles to the curved wall:
$$\text{TSA} = 2 \cdot (\text{Base Area}) + \text{CSA} = 2 \pi r^{2} + 2 \pi r h$$

Factor out the common `2πr`:
$$\text{TSA} = 2 \pi r (r + h)$$

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Memorize the table below. KTU lab viva and written exams ask direct one-mark questions like *"What is the formula for TSA of a cylinder?"* from this exact set.

| Quantity | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Base Area (single circle) | $\pi r^{2}$ | $r$ = radius | m² or cm² |
| **Volume** | $\pi r^{2} h$ | $r$ = radius, $h$ = height | m³ or cm³ |
| Curved Surface Area (CSA) | $2 \pi r h$ | $r$ = radius, $h$ = height | m² or cm² |
| **Total Surface Area (TSA)** | $2 \pi r^{2} + 2 \pi r h$ | $r$ = radius, $h$ = height | m² or cm² |
| **Total Surface Area (factored)** | $2 \pi r (r + h)$ | $r$ = radius, $h$ = height | m² or cm² |
| Circumference of base | $2 \pi r$ | $r$ = radius | m or cm |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Applications

| Field | Practical Use |
|---|---|
| **Civil Engineering** | Capacity of cylindrical water tanks, concrete pillars |
| **Mechanical Engineering** | Volume of fuel in cylindrical tanks, piston bore calculations |
| **Chemical Engineering** | Reactor vessel capacity, pipeline CSA for flow rate |
| **Electrical Engineering** | Cross-section of cables, cylindrical battery sizing |
| **Architecture** | Storage silos, domes, columns |

## 2.5 How the Python Program Maps to the Math

We will write **three distinct functions** for clean modularity:

| Python Function | Returns | Implements Formula |
|---|---|---|
| `cylinder_volume(r, h)` | Volume (float) | $\pi r^{2} h$ |
| `cylinder_csa(r, h)` | Curved Surface Area (float) | $2 \pi r h$ |
| `cylinder_tsa(r, h)` | Total Surface Area (float) | $2 \pi r (r + h)$ |

The main program simply takes input from the user and calls these functions.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Volume Formula

The volume of a cylinder is derived by stacking infinitely thin circular disks of radius `r` and infinitesimal thickness `dh` from height `0` to height `h`.

$$
\begin{aligned}
V &= \int_{0}^{h} \pi r^{2} \, dh \\[4pt]
V &= \pi r^{2} \int_{0}^{h} 1 \, dh \\[4pt]
V &= \pi r^{2} \big[ h \big]_{0}^{h} \\[4pt]
V &= \pi r^{2} (h - 0) \\[4pt]
V &= \pi r^{2} h
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic of each step:**
- Line 1 → Each horizontal slice is a disk of area `πr²`, infinitely thin.
- Line 2 → The radius `r` is constant, so it is pulled outside the integral.
- Line 3 → We integrate the constant `1` with respect to `h`.
- Line 4 → Evaluate the upper and lower limits of integration.
- Line 5 → Final compact volume formula.

> [!NOTE]
> For KTU, the integration derivation is **good to know**, but most lab-record marks come from the final boxed formula: $V = \pi r^{2} h$.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Curved Surface Area (CSA)

Imagine "unrolling" the curved wall of the cylinder. It flattens into a rectangle whose:
- One side equals the circumference of the circular base = $2 \pi r$.
- Other side equals the height of the cylinder = $h$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CSA} &= (\text{Length}) \times (\text{Breadth}) \\[4pt]
\text{CSA} &= (2 \pi r) \times (h) \\[4pt]
\text{CSA} &= 2 \pi r h
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.3 Derivation of the Total Surface Area (TSA)

The total surface consists of **two circular caps** (top and bottom) plus **one curved wall**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{TSA} &= \text{Top circle} + \text{Bottom circle} + \text{Curved wall} \\[4pt]
\text{TSA} &= \pi r^{2} + \pi r^{2} + 2 \pi r h \\[4pt]
\text{TSA} &= 2 \pi r^{2} + 2 \pi r h
\end{aligned}
$$

Now factor the common term $2 \pi r$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{TSA} &= 2 \pi r^{2} + 2 \pi r h \\[4pt]
\text{TSA} &= 2 \pi r \, (r + h)
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The factored form $\text{TSA} = 2 \pi r (r + h)$ is the **preferred form** in KTU answers because it is shorter, easier to write, and the examiner can see the factoring step explicitly.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (Verify Before Coding)

Let us plug in numbers to confirm our formulas are correct. Use $r = 7 \, \text{cm}$ and $h = 10 \, \text{cm}$. Take $\pi = 3.14159$.

**Volume calculation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V &= \pi r^{2} h \\[4pt]
V &= 3.14159 \times 7^{2} \times 10 \\[4pt]
V &= 3.14159 \times 49 \times 10 \\[4pt]
V &= 3.14159 \times 490 \\[4pt]
V &= 1539.38 \, \text{cm}^{3}
\end{aligned}
$$

**CSA calculation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CSA} &= 2 \pi r h \\[4pt]
\text{CSA} &= 2 \times 3.14159 \times 7 \times 10 \\[4pt]
\text{CSA} &= 439.82 \, \text{cm}^{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**TSA calculation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{TSA} &= 2 \pi r (r + h) \\[4pt]
\text{TSA} &= 2 \times 3.14159 \times 7 \times (7 + 10) \\[4pt]
\text{TSA} &= 2 \times 3.14159 \times 7 \times 17 \\[4pt]
\text{TSA} &= 747.70 \, \text{cm}^{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Analytical Verification Matrix:**

| Quantity | Computed Value | Units | Used Formula |
|---|---|---|---|
| Volume | 1539.38 | cm³ | $\pi r^{2} h$ |
| CSA | 439.82 | cm² | $2 \pi r h$ |
| TSA | 747.70 | cm² | $2 \pi r (r + h)$ |

This is exactly what our Python program should print when the user enters $r = 7$ and $h = 10$. (We will use `math.pi` for $\pi$, so the output will have more decimal places.)

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Cylinder Geometry (Reference Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["CYLINDER"] --> B["Top Circular Base"]
    A --> C["Bottom Circular Base"]
    A --> D["Curved Lateral Surface"]
    B --> B1["Radius = r"]
    B --> B2["Area = pi x r squared"]
    C --> C1["Radius = r"]
    C --> C2["Area = pi x r squared"]
    D --> D1["Height = h"]
    D --> D2["Unrolls to rectangle: 2 pi r by h"]
    D --> D3["CSA = 2 pi r h"]
```

## 4.2 Program Flowchart (Logical Flow)

The flowchart below captures the exact control flow of the Python program we will write. It uses the function-call approach as required by KTU.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start(["START"]) --> inputR["Read radius r from user"]
    inputR --> inputH["Read height h from user"]
    inputH --> callVol["Call cylinder_volume function with r and h"]
    callVol --> returnV["Function returns Volume value"]
    returnV --> callCSA["Call cylinder_csa function with r and h"]
    callCSA --> returnC["Function returns CSA value"]
    returnC --> callTSA["Call cylinder_tsa function with r and h"]
    callTSA --> returnT["Function returns TSA value"]
    returnT --> display["Display Volume, CSA, and TSA"]
    display --> stop(["END"])
```

## 4.3 Function-Level Architecture (Block Diagram)

This diagram explains how the main block and the three helper functions interact — a typical KTU expected diagram.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph MAIN["MAIN PROGRAM BLOCK"]
        M1["Read input r and h"]
        M2["Call volume, csa, tsa functions"]
        M3["Print all three results"]
    end

    subgraph FUNCS["USER DEFINED FUNCTIONS"]
        F1["cylinder_volume r h"]
        F2["cylinder_csa r h"]
        F3["cylinder_tsa r h"]
    end

    PI["math.pi constant"] --> F1
    PI --> F2
    PI --> F3

    M2 --> F1
    M2 --> F2
    M2 --> F3

    F1 --> M3
    F2 --> M3
    F3 --> M3
```

## 4.4 Data Flow Summary

| Step | Action | Direction |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | User enters `r` and `h` | Input → Main |
| 2 | Main passes `r, h` to each function | Main → Function |
| 3 | Function computes using `math.pi` and formula | Function internal |
| 4 | Function returns numeric value | Function → Main |
| 5 | Main prints result | Main → Output |

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Reference Python Program (Full Working Code)

> [!NOTE]
> This is the canonical program for this experiment. It satisfies the KTU requirement of "using function" by defining **three** user-defined functions and one main execution block.

```python
# Program: Surface Area and Volume of a Cylinder using Functions
# Course: PCCDL308 - Python and Statistical Modeling Lab
# KTU 2024 Scheme

import math  # To get an accurate value of pi

def cylinder_volume(r, h):
    """Return volume of cylinder given radius r and height h."""
    return math.pi * r * r * h

def cylinder_csa(r, h):
    """Return curved (lateral) surface area of cylinder."""
    return 2 * math.pi * r * h

def cylinder_tsa(r, h):
    """Return total surface area of cylinder."""
    return 2 * math.pi * r * (r + h)

def main():
    r = float(input("Enter the radius of the cylinder: "))
    h = float(input("Enter the height of the cylinder: "))

    v = cylinder_volume(r, h)
    csa = cylinder_csa(r, h)
    tsa = cylinder_tsa(r, h)

    print("\n--- Cylinder Results ---")
    print(f"Volume              = {v:.4f} cubic units")
    print(f"Curved Surface Area = {csa:.4f} sq. units")
    print(f"Total Surface Area  = {tsa:.4f} sq. units")

if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()
```

**Sample Run (with $r = 7$, $h = 10$):**

```
Enter the radius of the cylinder: 7
Enter the height of the cylinder: 10

--- Cylinder Results ---
Volume              = 1539.3804 cubic units
Curved Surface Area = 439.8230 sq. units
Total Surface Area  = 747.6990 sq. units
```

These numbers match our hand-computed values from Section 3.4, confirming the program is correct.

---

## 5.2 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**State the formula for the total surface area of a cylinder and explain the meaning of each term.**

**Model Answer:**

The total surface area of a cylinder of radius $r$ and height $h$ is given by:

$$\text{TSA} = 2 \pi r (r + h)$$

Meaning of each term:
- $r$ → radius of the circular base
- $h$ → height (distance between the two bases)
- $\pi$ → mathematical constant (≈ 3.14159)
- $2 \pi r$ → circumference of the base
- The term $2 \pi r h$ represents the **curved lateral surface**, and $2 \pi r^{2}$ represents the **two flat circular caps** (top and bottom).

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**What is the difference between Curved Surface Area (CSA) and Total Surface Area (TSA) of a cylinder?**

**Model Answer:**

- **Curved Surface Area (CSA)** considers only the curved side wall of the cylinder:
$$\text{CSA} = 2 \pi r h$$

- **Total Surface Area (TSA)** includes the CSA **plus** the areas of the two flat circular bases (top and bottom):
$$\text{TSA} = 2 \pi r h + 2 \pi r^{2} = 2 \pi r (r + h)$$

So TSA = CSA + area of two circular bases. The numerical difference is exactly $2 \pi r^{2}$.

---

## 5.3 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU Part B questions on lab topics typically come as **internal choice** — examiner offers two alternatives and you write only one. We provide both below.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Write a Python program using **user-defined functions** to compute the volume, curved surface area, and total surface area of a cylinder. Take radius and height as input from the user.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A cylindrical water tank has radius 3.5 m and height 6 m. Using the program from part (a), determine:
(i) Volume of the tank in cubic meters.
(ii) Total surface area in square meters.
(iii) If water costs ₹48 per cubic meter, what is the cost to fill the tank completely?

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** The complete program is given in Section 5.1 above. The three user-defined functions are `cylinder_volume(r, h)`, `cylinder_csa(r, h)`, and `cylinder_tsa(r, h)`. They each return a float value and are called from the `main()` function.

**(b)** Given $r = 3.5$ m and $h = 6$ m.

**(i) Volume:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V &= \pi r^{2} h \\
V &= 3.14159 \times (3.5)^{2} \times 6 \\
V &= 3.14159 \times 12.25 \times 6 \\
V &= 230.91 \, \text{m}^{3}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Total Surface Area:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{TSA} &= 2 \pi r (r + h) \\
\text{TSA} &= 2 \times 3.14159 \times 3.5 \times (3.5 + 6) \\
\text{TSA} &= 2 \times 3.14159 \times 3.5 \times 9.5 \\
\text{TSA} &= 208.92 \, \text{m}^{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Cost Calculation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Cost} &= V \times \text{Rate} \\
\text{Cost} &= 230.91 \times 48 \\
\text{Cost} &= \text{₹} \, 11{,}083.68
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answers:** (i) $230.91 \, \text{m}^{3}$ (ii) $208.92 \, \text{m}^{2}$ (iii) ₹ 11,083.68

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain with a neat diagram how a cylinder can be derived by integrating circular disks. Derive the volume formula $\pi r^{2} h$.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A closed cylindrical pipe of length 12 m has an outer radius of 0.4 m and an inner radius of 0.3 m. Write a Python function `pipe_surface_area(r_outer, r_inner, length)` that returns the **total external surface area** of the pipe. Also compute the value for the given data.

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** Refer to Section 3.1 of these notes. A cylinder can be visualized as the union of infinitely many infinitesimally thin circular disks stacked from height 0 to height $h$. Each disk has area $\pi r^{2}$ and thickness $dh$. Integrating gives:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V &= \int_{0}^{h} \pi r^{2} \, dh = \pi r^{2} h
\end{aligned}
$$

(Neat diagram: show a cylinder with horizontal slices labeled $dh$, total height $h$, and base radius $r$.)

**(b)** The total external surface area of a closed pipe consists of:
- Outer curved surface = $2 \pi r_{o} L$
- Inner curved surface = $2 \pi r_{i} L$
- Two annular (ring-shaped) end caps = $2 \cdot \pi (r_{o}^{2} - r_{i}^{2})$

```python
import math

def pipe_surface_area(r_outer, r_inner, length):
    outer_csa = 2 * math.pi * r_outer * length
    inner_csa = 2 * math.pi * r_inner * length
    end_caps  = 2 * math.pi * (r_outer**2 - r_inner**2)
    return outer_csa + inner_csa + end_caps

# Given data
r_o, r_i, L = 0.4, 0.3, 12
area = pipe_surface_area(r_o, r_i, L)
print(f"Total surface area of pipe = {area:.4f} sq.m")
```

**Numerical Computation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Outer CSA} &= 2 \pi (0.4)(12) = 30.159 \\
\text{Inner CSA} &= 2 \pi (0.3)(12) = 22.619 \\
\text{End caps}  &= 2 \pi (0.16 - 0.09) = 2 \pi (0.07) = 0.4398 \\
\text{Total}     &= 30.159 + 22.619 + 0.4398 = 53.218 \, \text{m}^{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer:** Total surface area $\approx 53.22 \, \text{m}^{2}$.

---

## 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes that cost marks in this experiment:**
> 1. **Forgetting `import math`** and using `3.14` instead — KTU prefers `math.pi` for accuracy.
> 2. **Writing everything inside one function** — the question explicitly says "using function" (singular or plural), so you MUST define at least one user-defined function with `def`. Marks are split for the function definition, the function call, and the output.
> 3. **Conflating CSA with TSA** — examiners check this with a direct 1-mark question. Always state both formulas clearly.
> 4. **Not handling units** — when input is in cm, output is in cm² and cm³; write units in the print statement.
> 5. **Using `int()` instead of `float()`** — radius and height are often decimals (e.g., 3.5), so always use `float(input(...))`.
> 6. **No docstring inside the function** — KTU appreciates a brief `"""description"""` line inside each function for modularity points.

---

## 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Exam Day:**

- A **cylinder** has two circular bases of equal radius and one curved lateral surface.
- **Volume:** $V = \pi r^{2} h$
- **Curved Surface Area (CSA):** $\text{CSA} = 2 \pi r h$
- **Total Surface Area (TSA):** $\text{TSA} = 2 \pi r (r + h)$ (factored form preferred)
- A user-defined function in Python is created using the keyword **`def`**.
- Use **`math.pi`** for accurate value of $\pi$ in Python.
- Use **`float(input(...))`** to read decimal numeric input.
- The program must have at least **one user-defined function** returning a value.
- Modular code = one function per formula = easy debugging and better marks.
- KTU viva questions on this experiment: *"What is CSA?", "What is the difference between CSA and TSA?", "Why use math.pi?", "What does `return` do?"*
- Always **print units** in the output (cm², cm³, m², m³) to show full clarity.
- Verify the program output against the hand-computed values from Section 3.4.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Write a program to replace a word by another word in a sentence
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCDL308 - PYTHON AND STATISTICAL MODELING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Write a program to replace a word by another word in a sentence

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**String Replacement** in Python refers to the operation of locating every occurrence of a specified *sub-string* (a word, phrase, or character sequence) within a parent string (sentence) and substituting it with a *target sub-string* of equal or different length. In the context of the KTU 2024 Scheme PCCDL308 lab, this operation is realized primarily through the built-in immutable string method `str.replace(old, new[, count])` defined in the Python Standard Library.

In formal CS terminology, the operation can be expressed as a *deterministic transducer* over the alphabet $\Sigma$ of characters. Given an input sentence $S = s_1 s_2 \ldots s_n$, a *pattern* $P = p_1 p_2 \ldots p_m$, and a *replacement* $R = r_1 r_2 \ldots r_k$, the operation produces an output string $S'$ where every non-overlapping occurrence of $P$ in $S$ is mapped to $R$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PCCDL308 — Module 2):**
> Under the KTU 2024 Scheme, students are expected to apply Python's inbuilt string manipulation methods to perform real-world text transformations. The `str.replace()` method, combined with user input via `input()`, forms the evaluation baseline for this experiment.

## 1.2 Intuitive Overview (Real-World Analogy)

Imagine you are a **copy editor** working for a newspaper. Your editor hands you a printed article and says, *"Everywhere the word 'colour' appears, change it to 'color'."* You pick up a red pen, scan line by line, strike out `colour`, and neatly write `color` above it. Once you are done, you hand the corrected page back. The **sentence** is the article, the **word to find** is `colour`, and the **replacement** is `color`.

In Python, this exact same manual workflow is automated by a single method call: `sentence.replace(old, new)`. The interpreter acts as your tireless digital copy editor.

## 1.3 Core Vocabulary for the Experiment

| KTU Term | Technical Meaning | Python Construct |
|---|---|---|
| **Sentence** | A sequence of characters terminated by punctuation | `str` object |
| **Word (Pattern)** | The sub-string to be searched for | `old` parameter |
| **Replacement** | The new sub-string to be inserted | `new` parameter |
| **Occurrence Count** | Number of non-overlapping matches found | Returned by `str.count()` |
| **Immutability** | Strings cannot be modified in-place | `S = S.replace(...)` reassignment |

> [!NOTE]
> **Immutability Principle:** Python strings are *immutable*. This means `str.replace()` does **not** modify the original string in memory; it *returns* a new string. This is a frequent KTU viva question and a common pitfall in board examinations.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown of `str.replace()`

The method signature is:

```python
str.replace(old, new, count)
```

Where:
- **`old`** *(str, mandatory)* — The sub-string to be replaced.
- **`new`** *(str, mandatory)* — The sub-string to replace it with.
- **`count`** *(int, optional)* — The maximum number of replacements to perform. If omitted, **all** non-overlapping occurrences are replaced.

The internal algorithm employs a *left-to-right greedy scan* of the input string. At each character index $i$, Python checks if the sub-string starting at $i$ of length $m$ (length of `old`) matches the pattern $P$. If yes, the output buffer receives $R$ and the scanner advances by $m$ characters. If no, the output buffer receives the single character $s_i$ and the scanner advances by $1$.

## 2.2 Algorithmic Logic Steps

1. **Input Phase:** Accept the original sentence $S$ from the user using `input()`.
2. **Input Phase:** Accept the target word $W_{old}$ to be replaced.
3. **Input Phase:** Accept the replacement word $W_{new}$.
4. **Search Phase:** Python internally scans $S$ from index $0$ to $n - m$ looking for $W_{old}$.
5. **Replace Phase:** Each non-overlapping match is substituted with $W_{new}$.
6. **Output Phase:** Print the newly constructed sentence $S'$.
7. **Safety Phase:** Optionally, count and report the number of replacements made.

## 2.3 KTU Formula / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Length of output string (worst case, no match) | $L_{out} = L_{in}$ | Identical to input |
| Length of output string (all matches replaced) | $L_{out} = L_{in} + k(L_{new} - L_{old})$ | Where $k$ is occurrence count |
| Non-overlapping condition | $i_{n+1} \geq i_n + m$ | No partial overlap allowed |
| Time complexity | $O(n \cdot m)$ | Naive scan, $n = \vert S\vert$, $m = \vert P\vert$ |
| Return type | `str` (new object) | Original `str` remains unchanged |
| Default replacement count | $\infty$ | Replaces every occurrence |
| String immutability | $S_{id} \neq (S.replace(\ldots))_{id}$ | Different memory addresses |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the formula matters:** The KTU board examiners often test whether students can predict the output length when a short word is replaced by a longer one (or vice-versa). Master the formula $L_{out} = L_{in} + k(L_{new} - L_{old})$.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

String replacement is foundational in:
- **Natural Language Processing (NLP):** Text normalization, lemmatization, and data cleaning pipelines.
- **Software Localization:** Translating UI strings (e.g., "Save" → "സംരക്ഷിക്കുക" in Malayalam).
- **Cybersecurity:** Sanitizing user inputs to prevent Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) by replacing `<script>` tags.
- **Compiler Design:** Token substitution within source code editors and IDEs.
- **Data Engineering (Pandas):** The `.str.replace()` method is a vectorized operation across millions of records in a DataFrame column.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example — Predicting Output Length

Let us derive the output string length for a concrete case.

**Given:**
- Input sentence: $S =$ `"I love Python. Python is great. Python is easy."`
- Word to find: $W_{old} =$ `"Python"` (length $L_{old} = 6$)
- Replacement: $W_{new} =$ `"Java"` (length $L_{new} = 4$)

**Step 1 — Identify the input length $L_{in}$:**

$$L_{in} = \text{len}(S) = 45 \text{ characters}$$

**Step 2 — Count non-overlapping occurrences $k$:**

Scanning left-to-right, we find "Python" at three distinct positions. The greedy left-to-right rule ensures no double-counting.

$$k = 3$$

**Step 3 — Apply the length formula:**

$$L_{out} = L_{in} + k \cdot (L_{new} - L_{old})$$

$$L_{out} = 45 + 3 \cdot (4 - 6)$$

$$L_{out} = 45 + 3 \cdot (-2)$$

$$L_{out} = 45 - 6 = 39 \text{ characters}$$

**Step 4 — Verify manually:** The output is `"I love Java. Java is great. Java is easy."` which has 39 characters. ✔

## 3.2 Full Reference Program (with Exhaustive Annotations)

The following is the canonical KTU board-style solution. Every line is explained for academic completeness.

```python
# Program: Replace a word with another word in a sentence
# Course  : PCCDL308 - Python and Statistical Modeling Lab
# Module  : 2 (Experiment 2)

# Step 1: Accept the original sentence from the user
sentence = input("Enter the original sentence: ")

# Step 2: Accept the word that needs to be replaced
old_word = input("Enter the word to be replaced: ")

# Step 3: Accept the replacement word
new_word = input("Enter the replacement word: ")

# Step 4: Safety check - confirm the word actually exists
if old_word in sentence:
    # Step 5a: Count occurrences for reporting
    occurrence_count = sentence.count(old_word)
    
    # Step 6a: Perform the replacement (str.replace returns a new string)
    modified_sentence = sentence.replace(old_word, new_word)
    
    # Step 7a: Display the results
    print("Original sentence :", sentence)
    print("Modified sentence :", modified_sentence)
    print("Number of replacements performed :", occurrence_count)
else:
    # Step 5b: Handle the no-match case gracefully
    print("The word '" + old_word + "' was not found in the sentence.")
    print("No replacement performed.")
```

### 3.3 Trace Table (Dry Run)

For input: sentence = `"Hello World"`, old = `"World"`, new = `"Python"`:

| Line | Statement | State of `sentence` | State of `old_word` | State of `new_word` | Output |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 4 | `input(...)` | `"Hello World"` | — | — | prompt shown |
| 7 | `input(...)` | `"Hello World"` | `"World"` | — | prompt shown |
| 10 | `input(...)` | `"Hello World"` | `"World"` | `"Python"` | prompt shown |
| 13 | `if old_word in sentence` | `"Hello World"` | `"World"` | `"Python"` | True → branch taken |
| 15 | `sentence.count(old_word)` | `"Hello World"` | `"World"` | `"Python"` | 1 |
| 18 | `sentence.replace(...)` | `"Hello World"` *(unchanged)* | `"World"` | `"Python"` | returns `"Hello Python"` |
| 21 | `print(...)` | `"Hello World"` | `"World"` | `"Python"` | 3 lines printed |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why does `sentence` remain unchanged at line 18?** Because strings in Python are **immutable**. The method creates a brand-new object in a different memory location. If the student forgets the assignment `modified_sentence = sentence.replace(...)` and prints `sentence` directly, marks will be deducted in the KTU board evaluation.

## 3.4 Advanced Variant — Limit Number of Replacements

Using the optional `count` parameter:

```python
sentence = "cat cat cat cat cat"
result = sentence.replace("cat", "dog", 2)
print(result)   # Output: dog dog cat cat cat
```

Mathematical model for limited replacement:

$$\text{replacements made} = \min(k, \text{count})$$

Where $k$ is the total number of occurrences in the string.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Program Flowchart (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A([Start]) --> B[Accept input sentence S]
    B --> C[Accept old word Wold]
    C --> D[Accept new word Wnew]
    D --> E{Wold exists in S?}
    E -- No --> F[Print: Word not found]
    F --> G([End])
    E -- Yes --> H[Count occurrences k of Wold]
    H --> I[Apply str.replace Wold to Wnew]
    I --> J[Store result in modified_sentence]
    J --> K[Print original sentence]
    K --> L[Print modified sentence]
    L --> M[Print replacement count k]
    M --> G
```

## 4.2 Functional Architecture — String Replacement Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    input1[User Input Layer] --> proc1[Preprocessing Tokenization]
    proc1 --> proc2[Pattern Matching Engine]
    proc2 --> proc3[Replacement Buffer Builder]
    proc3 --> output1[Output String Assembly]
    output1 --> display[Console Display Layer]
    
    subgraph Logic_Core
        proc1
        proc2
        proc3
    end
```

**Architecture Explanation:**

- **User Input Layer** — Captures raw keyboard input via `input()`.
- **Preprocessing Tokenization** — Python internally indexes the string into a character array.
- **Pattern Matching Engine** — Scans for matches of the `old` sub-string.
- **Replacement Buffer Builder** — Constructs the new string by appending either matched replacements or unmatched characters.
- **Output String Assembly** — Joins buffer contents into the final immutable `str` object.
- **Console Display Layer** — Renders the result via `print()`.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]
**What is the difference between `str.replace()` and `str.translate()` in Python? Mention one use-case for each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | `str.replace()` | `str.translate()` |
|---|---|---|
| Granularity | Operates on **sub-strings** (words/phrases) | Operates on **individual characters** |
| Speed | Slower for large texts | Faster (uses a translation table) |
| Use-case | Replacing full words (e.g., `"cat"` → `"dog"`) | Cipher substitution (e.g., `a → x`, `b → y`) |

`str.replace()` is used when we want to substitute an entire word or phrase, while `str.translate()` is preferred for character-level transformations like removing vowels or implementing a Caesar cipher.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Why does the following code fail to modify the original string? Explain with the concept of immutability.**

```python
s = "Hello World"
s.replace("World", "Python")
print(s)
```

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The code prints `Hello World` and not `Hello Python`. This is because Python strings are **immutable**, meaning their contents cannot be changed after creation. The `str.replace()` method does **not** modify the string in-place; instead, it returns a **new** string object containing the replacement. To fix this, the result must be assigned back:

```python
s = "Hello World"
s = s.replace("World", "Python")   # Reassignment captures the new object
print(s)                            # Output: Hello Python
```

This immutability property ensures thread safety and enables strings to be used as dictionary keys.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Note:** As per KTU ESE (End Semester Examination) 2024 Scheme, Module 2 carries internal choice. You must attempt **either** Question A **or** Question B.

### ✦ Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** Write a Python program to accept a sentence, a target word, and a replacement word from the user. Replace **all occurrences** of the target word with the replacement word and display the new sentence along with the number of replacements made. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Modify the program in part (a) so that it replaces only the **first occurrence** of the target word. Also handle the case where the target word is **not present** in the sentence. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

```python
sentence = input("Enter a sentence: ")
old_word = input("Enter the word to replace: ")
new_word = input("Enter the new word: ")

# Count occurrences
count = sentence.count(old_word)

# Perform replacement
new_sentence = sentence.replace(old_word, new_word)

# Display output
print("Original Sentence :", sentence)
print("Modified Sentence :", new_sentence)
print("Replacements made :", count)
```

**Step-by-step explanation (for board valuation):**
1. The `input()` function is used to accept string data from the user.
2. The `.count()` method returns the integer number of non-overlapping occurrences.
3. The `.replace()` method returns a new string with all matches substituted.
4. `print()` statements display the result.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

```python
sentence = input("Enter a sentence: ")
old_word = input("Enter the word to replace: ")
new_word = input("Enter the new word: ")

if old_word in sentence:
    # Replace only the first occurrence using count = 1
    new_sentence = sentence.replace(old_word, new_word, 1)
    print("Original Sentence :", sentence)
    print("Modified Sentence :", new_sentence)
    print("Only the first occurrence was replaced.")
else:
    print("The word '" + old_word + "' does not exist in the sentence.")
    print("No replacement was performed.")
```

**Key concepts tested:**
- Use of the `count` parameter to restrict replacements.
- Membership check using the `in` operator.
- Conditional logic with `if-else`.

---

### ✦ Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam — June 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the syntax and working of the `str.replace()` method in Python with a suitable example. Mention all three parameters. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Write a Python program to read a sentence and replace every occurrence of the word **"Python"** with **"Java"**. Additionally, display the length of the original sentence and the length of the modified sentence. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Syntax:**
```python
str.replace(old, new, count)
```

**Parameters:**
- **`old`** *(required)* — The sub-string to be replaced.
- **`new`** *(required)* — The sub-string to replace it with.
- **`count`** *(optional)* — Maximum number of replacements. Default is all occurrences.

**Working Example:**
```python
text = "apple apple apple"
result = text.replace("apple", "banana", 2)
print(result)    # Output: banana banana apple
```

**Explanation:** The interpreter scans the string from left to right. The first two matches of `"apple"` are swapped with `"banana"`. The third match is left untouched because `count=2` was specified. Since strings are immutable, `text` itself is unchanged and a new string `result` is returned.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

```python
sentence = input("Enter a sentence: ")

# Replace "Python" with "Java"
modified = sentence.replace("Python", "Java")

# Display lengths
print("Original sentence :", sentence)
print("Length of original sentence :", len(sentence))
print("Modified sentence :", modified)
print("Length of modified sentence :", len(modified))

# Optional: Apply the length formula
count = sentence.count("Python")
length_diff = len(modified) - len(sentence)
print("Number of replacements :", count)
print("Net length change :", length_diff)
```

**Verification using the formula:**
$$\text{length change} = k \cdot (L_{\text{Java}} - L_{\text{Python}}) = k \cdot (4 - 6) = -2k$$

If the original length is 50 and there are 3 occurrences of "Python", the new length is $50 + 3(-2) = 44$.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Forgetting to assign the result of `replace()` back** — Students often write `sentence.replace("a", "b")` directly inside `print()` and lose 2 marks.
> 2. **Case-sensitivity trap** — `"Python"` and `"python"` are treated as different words. Read the question carefully.
> 3. **Using `=` instead of `==`** in conditions.
> 4. **Not handling the no-match case** — The board expects graceful handling via `if-else`.
> 5. **Misunderstanding immutability** — A frequent 1-mark question in viva.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ Python strings are **immutable** — `replace()` returns a **new** string.
- ✅ Syntax: `str.replace(old, new, count)` where `count` is optional.
- ✅ Default behavior replaces **all** non-overlapping occurrences.
- ✅ Use `count` parameter to limit the number of replacements.
- ✅ Use `in` operator to check existence before replacing.
- ✅ Use `count()` method to find the number of matches.
- ✅ Length formula: $L_{out} = L_{in} + k \cdot (L_{new} - L_{old})$.
- ✅ Case-sensitive matching: `"Cat"` ≠ `"cat"`.
- ✅ Time complexity is $O(n \cdot m)$ using naive string matching.
- ✅ Real-world use: NLP cleaning, data sanitization, localization.
- ✅ Common KTU viva questions: immutability, return type, difference from `translate()`.
- ✅ Always reassign: `s = s.replace(old, new)` to capture the modified result.
- ✅ Graceful error handling with `if-else` earns full board marks.
- ✅ Print original and modified sentences side-by-side for clarity.
- ✅ Remember: Strings in Python are objects of the built-in `str` class.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Write a program to confirm the validity of an email id by verifying its format
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCDL308 - PYTHON AND STATISTICAL MODELING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Write a program to confirm the validity of an email id by verifying its format

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Email Validation** is the computational process of verifying whether a given string conforms to the standard syntactic rules defined by the Internet Engineering Task Force (IETF) in **RFC 5321** and **RFC 5322** for the format of an Internet Message address. In the context of the KTU 2024 Scheme Python Laboratory (PCCDL308), email validation is implemented as a Python program that examines the structural composition of an input string and returns a boolean outcome — `True` (valid) or `False` (invalid) — based on whether the string satisfies a defined set of pattern-matching rules.

A **standard email address** in its canonical form is defined as:

$$
\text{email} = \text{local-part} \; \texttt{@} \; \text{domain}
$$

where:
- **local-part** is the user identifier (left of the `@` symbol).
- **domain** is the mail server hostname (right of the `@` symbol), which itself contains a Top-Level Domain (TLD).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** This experiment falls under **Module 2 – String Operations and Regular Expressions** of PCCDL308. Students are expected to demonstrate the use of `re` (Regular Expressions), string slicing, the `split()` method, conditional logic, and user-defined functions. Validation must be done **strictly by format inspection**, not by network lookup (DNS/MX verification is out of scope).

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Postal Sorting Office

Imagine an email address as a **piece of physical mail** being delivered to a sorting office:

- The **local-part** (before `@`) is the **apartment number and resident name** written on the envelope. It tells the postal system *who* the mail is for inside a particular building.
- The **`@` symbol** is the **building's main entrance** — the single, unambiguous gateway separating "inside this building" from "outside in the city".
- The **domain** (after `@`) is the **building's full street address**, ending with a **country code or area type** (the TLD) — for example, `.com`, `.in`, `.org`, `.edu`.

The postal worker (your Python program) doesn't know who actually lives in the apartment — they only check the *envelope's format*:
- Is there **exactly one** entrance (`@`)?
- Does the apartment number contain only **allowed characters** (letters, digits, dots, underscores)?
- Is the street address **non-empty** and **ends with a valid area code** (TLD of at least 2 alphabetic characters)?

If all these format checks pass, the envelope is "structurally valid" — even if the resident is fictional. **This is exactly what format-based email validation does in Python.**

> [!NOTE]
