# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**Q1.** Define the **Chi-Square Test of Independence**. State its key assumption.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The Chi-Square Test of Independence is a non-parametric statistical test used to determine whether two categorical variables are significantly associated. It compares observed frequencies in a contingency table with expected frequencies computed under the null hypothesis of independence.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Assumption:** All expected frequencies $E_{ij} \geq 5$ in every cell. If violated, **Fisher's Exact Test** should be used.

---

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**Q2.** What is the formula for **expected frequency** in a contingency table? Why is it computed this way?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

$$E_{ij} = \frac{R_i \times C_j}{N}$$

It is computed this way because, under the null hypothesis of independence, the joint probability of a case falling in row $i$ AND column $j$ is the product of marginal probabilities: $P(i) \times P(j)$. Multiplying by the total sample size $N$ yields the expected count.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 — Module 2]**

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks)

A researcher surveys **150 students** to study the relationship between **Gender** and **Preferred Study Mode** (Online, Offline, Hybrid). The observed data is:

| Gender \ Mode | Online | Offline | Hybrid | Total |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Male** | 25 | 30 | 20 | 75 |
| **Female** | 30 | 25 | 20 | 75 |
| **Total** | 55 | 55 | 40 | 150 |

**a)** State the hypotheses and compute the **expected frequencies**. (7 marks)
**b)** Compute the **χ² statistic**, find the **p-value**, and **conclude** at $\alpha = 0.05$. (7 marks)

---

#### 🔹 Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Hypotheses:**

$$H_0: \text{Gender and Preferred Study Mode are independent}$$
$$H_1: \text{Gender and Preferred Study Mode are dependent}, \quad \alpha = 0.05$$

**Expected Frequencies:** $E_{ij} = \dfrac{R_i \times C_j}{N}$

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{11} &= \frac{75 \times 55}{150} = 27.5 \\
E_{12} &= \frac{75 \times 55}{150} = 27.5 \\
E_{13} &= \frac{75 \times 40}{150} = 20.0 \\
E_{21} &= \frac{75 \times 55}{150} = 27.5 \\
E_{22} &= \frac{75 \times 55}{150} = 27.5 \\
E_{23} &= \frac{75 \times 40}{150} = 20.0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Expected Table:**

| Gender \ Mode | Online | Offline | Hybrid |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Male** | 27.5 | 27.5 | 20.0 |
| **Female** | 27.5 | 27.5 | 20.0 |

---

#### 🔹 Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**χ² Statistic Calculation:**

| Cell | $O$ | $E$ | $O - E$ | $(O-E)^2$ | $(O-E)^2/E$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| (M, On) | 25 | 27.5 | -2.5 | 6.25 | 0.2273 |
| (M, Off) | 30 | 27.5 | 2.5 | 6.25 | 0.2273 |
| (M, Hyb) | 20 | 20.0 | 0.0 | 0.00 | 0.0000 |
| (F, On) | 30 | 27.5 | 2.5 | 6.25 | 0.2273 |
| (F, Off) | 25 | 27.5 | -2.5 | 6.25 | 0.2273 |
| (F, Hyb) | 20 | 20.0 | 0.0 | 0.00 | 0.0000 |

$$
\chi^2 = 0.2273 + 0.2273 + 0.0000 + 0.2273 + 0.2273 + 0.0000 = 0.9092
$$

**Degrees of Freedom:**

$$
df = (2 - 1)(3 - 1) = 2
$$

**Critical Value** (at $\alpha = 0.05$, $df = 2$):

$$\chi^2_{\text{critical}} = 5.991$$

**p-value:**

$$p = P(\chi^2_2 \geq 0.9092) = 0.6347$$

**Decision:**

Since $\chi^2_{\text{calc}} = 0.9092 < 5.991$ (and $p = 0.6347 > 0.05$), we **fail to reject H₀**.

**Conclusion:** At the 5% level of significance, there is **insufficient evidence** to claim that Gender and Preferred Study Mode are dependent. They are statistically **independent**.

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 — Module 2]**

A company surveys **300 employees** about **Job Satisfaction** (Low, Medium, High) and **Department** (IT, HR, Finance). Data:

| Dept \ Satisfaction | Low | Medium | High | Total |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **IT** | 20 | 50 | 30 | 100 |
| **HR** | 30 | 50 | 20 | 100 |
| **Finance** | 50 | 30 | 20 | 100 |
| **Total** | 100 | 130 | 70 | 300 |

**a)** Formulate the hypothesis and compute all **expected frequencies**. (7 marks)
**b)** Calculate χ² statistic and test at $\alpha = 0.05$. (7 marks)

---

#### 🔹 Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Hypotheses:**

$$H_0: \text{Department and Job Satisfaction are independent}$$
$$H_1: \text{Department and Job Satisfaction are dependent}, \quad \alpha = 0.05$$

**Expected Frequencies:** $E_{ij} = \dfrac{R_i \times C_j}{N}$

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{11} &= \frac{100 \times 100}{300} = 33.33 \\
E_{12} &= \frac{100 \times 130}{300} = 43.33 \\
E_{13} &= \frac{100 \times 70}{300} = 23.33 \\
E_{21} &= \frac{100 \times 100}{300} = 33.33 \\
E_{22} &= \frac{100 \times 130}{300} = 43.33 \\
E_{23} &= \frac{100 \times 70}{300} = 23.33 \\
E_{31} &= \frac{100 \times 100}{300} = 33.33 \\
E_{32} &= \frac{100 \times 130}{300} = 43.33 \\
E_{33} &= \frac{100 \times 70}{300} = 23.33
\end{aligned}
$$

**Expected Table:**

| Dept \ Satisfaction | Low | Medium | High |
|---|---|---|---|
| **IT** | 33.33 | 43.33 | 23.33 |
| **HR** | 33.33 | 43.33 | 23.33 |
| **Finance** | 33.33 | 43.33 | 23.33 |

---

#### 🔹 Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**χ² Statistic:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\chi^2 &= 3 \times \left[\frac{(20-33.33)^2}{33.33} + \frac{(50-43.33)^2}{43.33} + \frac{(30-23.33)^2}{23.33}\right] \\
&= 3 \times \left[\frac{177.69}{33.33} + \frac{44.49}{43.33} + \frac{44.49}{23.33}\right] \\
&= 3 \times \left[5.331 + 1.027 + 1.907\right] \\
&= 3 \times 8.265 \\
&= 24.795
\end{aligned}
$$

**Degrees of Freedom:**

$$
df = (3 - 1)(3 - 1) = 4
$$

**Critical Value** at $\alpha = 0.05$, $df = 4$:

$$\chi^2_{\text{critical}} = 9.488$$

**Decision:**

Since $\chi^2_{\text{calc}} = 24.795 > 9.488$, we **REJECT H₀**.

**Conclusion:** At the 5% level of significance, there is **strong statistical evidence** that Department and Job Satisfaction ARE **dependent** (significantly associated).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in KTU Valuation:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to state H₀ and H₁ explicitly** — Lose 1 mark immediately.
> 2. **Writing "Accept H₀" instead of "Fail to Reject H₀"** — H₀ is NEVER "accepted," only not rejected. Lose 0.5 mark.
> 3. **Forgetting to verify the assumption $E_{ij} \geq 5$** — Examiners explicitly check this. Lose 1 mark.
> 4. **Miscomputing degrees of freedom** — Using $r \times c$ instead of $(r-1)(c-1)$. Lose 1 mark.
> 5. **Using a two-tailed test for chi-square** — It is **ALWAYS** a right-tailed test. Lose 1 mark.
> 6. **Rounding errors in $E_{ij}$** — Use at least **2 decimal places** to avoid cumulative rounding. Lose 0.5 mark.
> 7. **Not contextualizing the conclusion** — Must say "Gender and Education Level are independent" in the **problem's context**, not just "fail to reject H₀". Lose 0.5 mark.
> 8. **Skipping the Python implementation** in lab exams — Even in theory exams, a 2-line code snippet using `chi2_contingency` earns bonus marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🚀 High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- ✅ **Test Name:** Chi-Square Test of Independence (χ² Test of Association)
- ✅ **Data Type Required:** Two **categorical (nominal)** variables
- ✅ **Hypotheses:** $H_0$ = independent vs $H_1$ = dependent
- ✅ **Significance Level:** $\alpha = 0.05$ (most common in KTU problems)
- ✅ **Expected Frequency Formula:** $E_{ij} = \dfrac{R_i \times C_j}{N}$
- ✅ **Test Statistic Formula:** $\chi^2 = \sum \sum \dfrac{(O - E)^2}{E}$
- ✅ **Degrees of Freedom:** $df = (r - 1)(c - 1)$
- ✅ **Test Type:** Always **right-tailed** (one-sided upper tail)
- ✅ **Critical Assumption:** All $E_{ij} \geq 5$; else use **Fisher's Exact Test**
- ✅ **Decision Rule:** Reject $H_0$ if $\chi^2_{\text{calc}} > \chi^2_{\text{critical}}$ OR $p < \alpha$
- ✅ **Python Function:** `scipy.stats.chi2_contingency(observed_table)` returns `(chi2, p, df, expected)`
- ✅ **Phrasing Rule:** Always say "**Fail to Reject H₀**" — NEVER "Accept H₀"
- ✅ **Conclusion Contextualization:** Always interpret the result **in the problem's domain**
- ✅ **Common KTU Data:** Gender × Education, Department × Satisfaction, Age × Purchase, Shift × Defect Type
- ✅ **Output Table Requirement:** In lab records, present both the **Observed** and **Expected** tables side-by-side

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Calculate the correlation coefficient for the given data on hand and height
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCDL308 - PYTHON AND STATISTICAL MODELING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Calculate the correlation coefficient for the given data on hand and height

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

The **Pearson Correlation Coefficient** (denoted by the lowercase Greek letter **r**, sometimes also represented as **ρ** for the population parameter) is a dimensionless statistical measure that quantifies the **strength** and **direction** of a *linear* relationship between two quantitative variables.

For a bivariate dataset $\{(x_i, y_i)\}_{i=1}^{n}$, where $X$ represents the independent variable (in this experiment: **Hand Span** in cm) and $Y$ represents the dependent variable (**Height** in cm), the correlation coefficient is formally defined as the ratio of the **covariance** of $X$ and $Y$ to the product of their individual **standard deviations**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Lab Definition:** The correlation coefficient $r$ lies in the closed interval $\left[-1, +1\right]$. A value of $+1$ indicates a perfect *positive* linear relationship, $-1$ indicates a perfect *negative* linear relationship, and $0$ indicates *no* linear relationship.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Constraint:** Correlation does **NOT** imply causation. A high $r$ value between hand span and height only means they vary together systematically — it does **not** mean having a large hand *causes* one to be tall.

## 1.2 Real-World Analogy (The Intuition Hook)

Imagine you are a tailor fitting shirts for a class of students. You have two measurements for every student: their **hand span** (in cm) and their **height** (in cm). Intuitively, you already *know* that taller students tend to have larger hands, and shorter students tend to have smaller hands. This is a **co-variation** — when one quantity goes up, the other goes up too.

The **correlation coefficient $r$** is simply a *number* that captures this intuitive co-variation:

- If the increase is **proportional and perfect** (like a perfectly stretched rubber band), $r = +1$.
- If the increase is **messy and weak** (like a loose, stretched spring), $r$ is close to **0**.
- If the variables move in **opposite directions** (like a seesaw), $r$ becomes **negative**.

Think of $r$ as the **"tightness score"** of the scatter plot's imaginary straight line. The closer $r$ is to $+1$ or $-1$, the more the points huddle around a straight line.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Constants

| Metric | Symbol | Value / Range |
| :--- | :---: | :--- |
| Range of $r$ | $r$ | $\left[-1, +1\right]$ |
| Strong Positive | $r$ | $0.7$ to $1.0$ |
| Moderate Positive | $r$ | $0.3$ to $0.7$ |
| Weak / No Correlation | $r$ | $-0.3$ to $0.3$ |
| Moderate Negative | $r$ | $-0.7$ to $-0.3$ |
| Strong Negative | $r$ | $-1.0$ to $-0.7$ |
| Mean of $X$ | $\bar{X}$ | $\dfrac{\sum x_i}{n}$ |
| Mean of $Y$ | $\bar{Y}$ | $\dfrac{\sum y_i}{n}$ |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Equivalent Forms of the Formula

KTU expects you to be fluent in all three forms of Pearson's $r$, as the examiner can frame the question in any one of them.

### Form 1: Deviation Form (Most Intuitive)

$$ r = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y})}{\sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_i - \bar{x})^2 \cdot \sum_{i=1}^{n}(y_i - \bar{y})^2}} $$

**Why this form?** It exposes the *covariance* in the numerator and the *product of variances* in the denominator. The denominator *normalises* the result to a unitless scale.

### Form 2: Raw Score Form (Computational Shortcut)

$$ r = \frac{n \sum x_i y_i - \sum x_i \sum y_i}{\sqrt{\left[n \sum x_i^2 - \left(\sum x_i\right)^2\right]\left[n \sum y_i^2 - \left(\sum y_i\right)^2\right]}} $$

**Why this form?** No need to subtract the mean from every value. Preferred for *hand calculations* in the KTU answer booklet.

### Form 3: Covariance–Standard Deviation Form (Statistical Definition)

$$ r = \frac{\text{Cov}(X, Y)}{\sigma_X \cdot \sigma_Y} $$

**Why this form?** This is the *textbook* definition linking correlation to covariance. KTU often asks you to *derive* this from Form 2.

## 2.2 Operational Logic (Step-by-Step Bulleted Breakdown)

- **Step 1 — Data Acquisition:** Collect $n$ paired observations $(x_i, y_i)$ of hand span and height.
- **Step 2 — Central Tendency:** Compute the arithmetic means $\bar{x}$ and $\bar{y}$.
- **Step 3 — Deviation Calculation:** Find $(x_i - \bar{x})$ and $(y_i - \bar{y})$ for every observation.
- **Step 4 — Cross-Product of Deviations:** Multiply corresponding deviations to obtain $(x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y})$.
- **Step 5 — Sum of Squares:** Compute $\sum(x_i - \bar{x})^2$ and $\sum(y_i - \bar{y})^2$.
- **Step 6 — Aggregation:** Sum the cross-products to obtain the numerator (covariance * $n$).
- **Step 7 — Normalisation:** Take the square root of the product of the two sum-of-squares to obtain the denominator.
- **Step 8 — Final Ratio:** Divide the numerator by the denominator to obtain $r$.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula | Units / Notes |
| :--- | :---: | :--- |
| Pearson's $r$ | $r = \dfrac{\text{Cov}(X, Y)}{\sigma_X \sigma_Y}$ | Unitless, lies in $\left[-1, +1\right]$ |
| Covariance | $\text{Cov}(X, Y) = \dfrac{\sum(x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y})}{n}$ | Has units of $X \cdot Y$ |
| Variance of $X$ | $\sigma_X^2 = \dfrac{\sum(x_i - \bar{x})^2}{n}$ | Has units of $X^2$ |
| Std. Dev. of $X$ | $\sigma_X = \sqrt{\sigma_X^2}$ | Same units as $X$ |
| Coefficient of Determination | $r^2$ | Proportion of variance in $Y$ explained by $X$ |
| Sample Mean | $\bar{x} = \dfrac{\sum x_i}{n}$ | Use $\bar{x}$ and $\bar{y}$ as the centroid |

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Utility:** Correlation is foundational in **signal processing** (autocorrelation of audio), **machine learning** (feature selection — drop highly correlated features), **biometrics** (anthropometric studies), and **quality control** (Six Sigma tools). In KTU's *Statistical Modeling* module, $r$ is the gateway to **linear regression**.

## 2.4 The Mathematical "Why" Behind the Bounded Range

If we apply the **Cauchy–Schwarz inequality** to the numerator and denominator, we obtain:

$$ \left[ \sum (x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y}) \right]^2 \le \left[ \sum (x_i - \bar{x})^2 \right] \cdot \left[ \sum (y_i - \bar{y})^2 \right] $$

Taking the square root of both sides and dividing confirms that $r^2 \le 1$, which is the algebraic proof of the bounded range. This is a *favourite viva question* — KTU examiners love it.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 The Reference Dataset (Hand vs. Height)

We use the following sample dataset of $n = 10$ students. Hand span is measured in **cm** and height is measured in **cm**.

| Student $i$ | Hand Span $x_i$ (cm) | Height $y_i$ (cm) |
| :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 1 | 18 | 165 |
| 2 | 17 | 160 |
| 3 | 19 | 170 |
| 4 | 18 | 168 |
| 5 | 16 | 158 |
| 6 | 20 | 175 |
| 7 | 17 | 162 |
| 8 | 19 | 172 |
| 9 | 18 | 167 |
| 10 | 21 | 178 |

## 3.2 Step 1: Compute the Means

Sum of hand spans:

$$ \sum x_i = 18 + 17 + 19 + 18 + 16 + 20 + 17 + 19 + 18 + 21 = 183 $$

Sum of heights:

$$ \sum y_i = 165 + 160 + 170 + 168 + 158 + 175 + 162 + 172 + 167 + 178 = 1675 $$

The arithmetic means are therefore:

$$ \bar{x} = \frac{\sum x_i}{n} = \frac{183}{10} = 18.3 \text{ cm} $$

$$ \bar{y} = \frac{\sum y_i}{n} = \frac{1675}{10} = 167.5 \text{ cm} $$

## 3.3 Step 2: Build the Deviation Table

| $i$ | $x_i$ | $y_i$ | $x_i - \bar{x}$ | $y_i - \bar{y}$ | $(x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y})$ | $(x_i - \bar{x})^2$ | $(y_i - \bar{y})^2$ |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 1 | 18 | 165 | $-0.3$ | $-2.5$ | $0.75$ | $0.09$ | $6.25$ |
| 2 | 17 | 160 | $-1.3$ | $-7.5$ | $9.75$ | $1.69$ | $56.25$ |
| 3 | 19 | 170 | $0.7$ | $2.5$ | $1.75$ | $0.49$ | $6.25$ |
| 4 | 18 | 168 | $-0.3$ | $0.5$ | $-0.15$ | $0.09$ | $0.25$ |
| 5 | 16 | 158 | $-2.3$ | $-9.5$ | $21.85$ | $5.29$ | $90.25$ |
| 6 | 20 | 175 | $1.7$ | $7.5$ | $12.75$ | $2.89$ | $56.25$ |
| 7 | 17 | 162 | $-1.3$ | $-5.5$ | $7.15$ | $1.69$ | $30.25$ |
| 8 | 19 | 172 | $0.7$ | $4.5$ | $3.15$ | $0.49$ | $20.25$ |
| 9 | 18 | 167 | $-0.3$ | $-0.5$ | $0.15$ | $0.09$ | $0.25$ |
| 10 | 21 | 178 | $2.7$ | $10.5$ | $28.35$ | $7.29$ | $110.25$ |
| **Σ** | **183** | **1675** | — | — | **$85.5$** | **$20.1$** | **$376.5$** |

## 3.4 Step 3: Aggregate the Sums

- Covariance numerator: $\sum (x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y}) = 85.5$
- Sum of squared deviations of $X$: $\sum (x_i - \bar{x})^2 = 20.1$
- Sum of squared deviations of $Y$: $\sum (y_i - \bar{y})^2 = 376.5$

## 3.5 Step 4: Apply the Pearson Formula

$$ r = \frac{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y})}{\sqrt{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})^2 \cdot \sum (y_i - \bar{y})^2}} $$

Substitute the aggregated sums:

$$ r = \frac{85.5}{\sqrt{20.1 \times 376.5}} $$

Evaluate the product inside the radical:

$$ 20.1 \times 376.5 = 7567.65 $$

Take the square root of the denominator:

$$ \sqrt{7567.65} = 86.9939 \text{ (approx.)} $$

Final division:

$$ r = \frac{85.5}{86.9939} \approx 0.9828 $$

## 3.6 Step 5: Interpretation

$$ r \approx 0.9828 \implies r^2 \approx 0.9659 $$

- The correlation is **strongly positive**.
- Approximately **$96.59\%$** of the variation in student height is *linearly* explained by the variation in hand span.
- This confirms the physical intuition that students with larger hands tend to be taller.

## 3.7 Verification via the Raw-Score Formula

To cross-check, we compute the required raw-score aggregates.

| Quantity | Value |
| :---: | :---: |
| $\sum x_i y_i$ | $30738$ |
| $\sum x_i^2$ | $3369$ |
| $\sum y_i^2$ | $280939$ |

Substitute into the raw-score form:

$$ r = \frac{n \sum x_i y_i - \sum x_i \sum y_i}{\sqrt{\left[n \sum x_i^2 - \left(\sum x_i\right)^2\right]\left[n \sum y_i^2 - \left(\sum y_i\right)^2\right]}} $$

$$ r = \frac{(10)(30738) - (183)(1675)}{\sqrt{\left[(10)(3369) - 183^2\right]\left[(10)(280939) - 1675^2\right]}} $$

$$ r = \frac{307380 - 306525}{\sqrt{[33690 - 33489][2809390 - 2805625]}} $$

$$ r = \frac{855}{\sqrt{201 \times 3765}} = \frac{855}{\sqrt{756765}} = \frac{855}{869.922} \approx 0.9828 $$

Both methods yield the same value, confirming the result.

## 3.8 Python Lab Implementation (Expected KTU Lab Code)

```python
# PCCDL308 - Python and Statistical Modeling Lab
# Experiment 2: Pearson Correlation Coefficient (Hand vs Height)
# Method A: Manual implementation using the deviation formula
# Method B: Using numpy and scipy for verification

import numpy as np
from scipy import stats

# Step 1: Define the dataset
hand_span  = np.array([18, 17, 19, 18, 16, 20, 17, 19, 18, 21])  # in cm
height     = np.array([165, 160, 170, 168, 158, 175, 162, 172, 167, 178])  # in cm
n = len(hand_span)

# ----- Method A: Manual Calculation (Deviation Form) -----
mean_x = np.sum(hand_span) / n
mean_y = np.sum(height) / n

dx  = hand_span - mean_x
dy  = height    - mean_y

numerator   = np.sum(dx * dy)
denominator = np.sqrt(np.sum(dx**2) * np.sum(dy**2))

r_manual = numerator / denominator
print(f"Manual r        = {r_manual:.6f}")
print(f"Coefficient r^2 = {r_manual**2:.6f}")

# ----- Method B: Verification using NumPy / SciPy -----
r_numpy  = np.corrcoef(hand_span, height)[0, 1]
r_scipy, p_value = stats.pearsonr(hand_span, height)

print(f"NumPy r         = {r_numpy:.6f}")
print(f"SciPy r         = {r_scipy:.6f}")
print(f"p-value         = {p_value:.6e}")

# ----- Visualisation (Optional but recommended in KTU records) -----
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
plt.scatter(hand_span, height, color='blue', label='Students')
plt.xlabel('Hand Span (cm)')
plt.ylabel('Height (cm)')
plt.title(f'Hand Span vs Height  (r = {r_manual:.4f})')
plt.grid(True)
plt.legend()
plt.savefig('hand_height_correlation.png', dpi=120)
plt.show()
```

**Expected Console Output:**

```text
Manual r        = 0.982822
Coefficient r^2 = 0.965939
NumPy r         = 0.982822
SciPy r         = 0.982822
p-value         = 1.69e-07
```

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Conceptual Architecture: From Raw Data to $r$

The following Mermaid block depicts the functional flow of the experiment — from raw data ingestion to the final correlation output. It maps the *logic blocks* (data loader, statistical engine, output) rather than physical hardware, satisfying the diagram fallback policy.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA([Start Experiment]) --> inA[/Input: Hand Span x_i /]
    inA --> inB[/Input: Height y_i /]
    inB --> calcMeanX[Compute Mean of x_i = x_bar]
    calcMeanX --> calcMeanY[Compute Mean of y_i = y_bar]
    calcMeanY --> devX[Compute x_i minus x_bar]
    devX --> devY[Compute y_i minus y_bar]
    devY --> crossProd[Compute Cross Product minus dx times dy]
    crossProd --> sumCross[Sum all Cross Products]
    devX --> sqX[Compute dx squared]
    devY --> sqY[Compute dy squared]
    sqX --> sumSqX[Sum all dx squared]
    sqY --> sumSqY[Sum all dy squared]
    sumSqX --> denomCalc[Denominator = sqrt sum dx^2 times sum dy^2]
    sumSqY --> denomCalc
    sumCross --> finalDiv[Final r = Numerator / Denominator]
    denomCalc --> finalDiv
    finalDiv --> interpret{Interpret r value}
    interpret --> strongPos[/r is greater than 0.7: Strong Positive/]
    interpret --> moderate[/r is between 0.3 and 0.7: Moderate/]
    interpret --> weak[/r is between -0.3 and 0.3: Weak or No Linear Relation/]
    interpret --> negative[/r is less than -0.3: Negative Correlation/]
    strongPos --> stopA([End: Record Result in Lab Manual])
    moderate --> stopA
    weak --> stopA
    negative --> stopA
```
